message:
fcp-sandbox: enforce RLIMIT_AS alongside RLIMIT_DATA and stabilize WASI preopens

Both LinuxSandbox and MacOsSandbox previously set RLIMIT_DATA to the
policy's memory_limit_bytes but explicitly left RLIMIT_AS unbounded,
justified in comments by the concern that modern runtimes (Go, and
Rust with certain allocators) reserve large virtual address spaces up
front and would crash on startup if RLIMIT_AS were tight.

That tradeoff was wrong for the threat model fcp-sandbox is defending
against: RLIMIT_DATA only bounds the traditional heap/data segment,
not anonymous mmap-backed allocations. A guest could trivially bypass
the memory budget by issuing repeated mmap() calls for private
anonymous regions, which consume committed physical memory without
ever touching the data segment. The "we can't enforce memory" escape
hatch swallowed real sandbox escape potential to dodge a startup
inconvenience.

This change sets RLIMIT_AS = memory_limit_bytes in both the in-process
apply_rlimits path and the child-process pre_exec path on Linux and
macOS. Callers who need headroom for large virtual reservations must
raise memory_limit_bytes; they can no longer implicitly rely on
mmap-based allocation being outside the budget. The comments have
been rewritten to describe the new behavior and the mmap-bypass
rationale so a future reader does not reintroduce the old
justification.

Separately, WASI runtime preopen configuration (crates/fcp-sandbox/
src/wasi.rs) had several subtle hazards:

  - Preopen paths were passed to preopened_dir as the non-canonical
    user-supplied PathBuf, letting a symlink-controlled component in
    the host path widen guest access to siblings of the intended
    directory.
  - writable_paths creation used `let _ = std::fs::create_dir_all(...)`
    and then silently ignored failures behind a subsequent is_dir()
    check, masking permission errors as "not a directory" and hiding
    real misconfiguration.
  - A preopen whose parent became a symlink between is_dir() and the
    actual preopen call could race from a regular directory to an
    attacker-controlled link.

Introduce prepare_preopened_dir() which:

  - Calls ensure_preopen_path_is_stable() (existing helper) to reject
    symlinked or unstable preopen targets up front.
  - For writable paths, creates the directory eagerly and surfaces any
    I/O failure as WasiError::FsAccessDenied rather than silently
    skipping.
  - Returns None only for non-existent read-only paths or paths that
    are not directories after stabilization, skipping those preopens
    rather than producing a bogus mount.
  - Canonicalizes the stable path and hands the canonical PathBuf to
    preopened_dir, so the mount cannot be redirected post-check.

Call sites in WasiRuntime::build_preopens are rewritten to use the new
helper; the guest-facing mount path continues to be the original
user-facing string.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
